title: Why demining the Strait of Hormuz will be difficult – but also presents an opportunity
gist: Following the US-Iran deal, roughly 80 hard-to-detect Iranian mines and rerouted, congested shipping lanes are keeping Strait of Hormuz traffic at about 25 ships/day versus a pre-war average of 125, and full reopening depends on a fragile, internationally coordinated (UK-France-led) demining effort that doubles as a test of US-Iran de-escalation.

themes.theme: Scale and nature of the mine threat

insights.claim: Iran has not publicly confirmed how many mines it laid in the Strait of Hormuz, but estimates suggest about 80 mines, primarily Maham 3 and Maham 7 high-explosive blast mines that resist detection by deviating sonar waves used by minesweepers.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The ~80-mine estimate and the sonar-evading design of Maham mines set a concrete, technically difficult baseline for how long full reopening of the Strait could take, relevant to oil tanker insurance and freight-rate forecasting.
insights.quote: Maham mines resist detection by deviating sonar waves used by minesweepers, making them extremely difficult to detect and remove.
insights.locator: Mines in the Strait of Hormuz section

insights.claim: Before the war, Iran had an estimated stock of 5,000-6,000 sea mines, a significant share domestically produced, and some analysts believe Tehran has retained 80-90 per cent of its small boats and minelaying vessels.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A large retained mine stockpile and minelaying capacity means the risk of renewed mining is not eliminated even after current demining, a persistent tail risk for shipping and oil markets.

insights.claim: There are four suspected but unconfirmed minefield areas: the western entrance to the Strait, the central eastbound shipping lane, the central westbound shipping lane, and the eastern exit toward the Gulf of Oman.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Mapping the suspected minefields to the main shipping lanes shows why the primary central channel — the most efficient route — remains blocked, keeping shipping costs and transit times elevated.

insights.claim: With the main central route effectively closed by mines, two passable routes now exist: an Iranian-established northern route hugging its coast (raising seizure and toll risk) and a US-established southern route along the Omani coast; both are narrow and push ships to shallow edges, raising grounding risk.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The northern route creates an explicit mechanism for Iran to seize ships or levy tolls, a direct commercial and political risk for shippers and a potential new revenue/leverage tool for Tehran.
insights.locator: Since the main central route paragraph

insights.claim: Daily Strait of Hormuz transits have averaged about 25 ships in recent days, compared to a pre-war daily average of around 125, with 500-600 ships still stranded in the Persian Gulf; traffic cannot return to pre-war levels without reopening the main central channel.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: An 80% drop in daily transits (125 to 25) with 500-600 ships stranded is a hard, quantifiable capacity constraint directly relevant to global oil supply, freight rates and tanker demurrage costs.
insights.quote: in recent days only an average of 25 ships have transited daily, compared to the pre-war daily average of around 125

insights.claim: Ships are turning off AIS location signals to avoid detection by Iranian forces on the southern route, and this 'going dark' practice — also used in sanctions evasion and by Iran's shadow fleet — increases collision risk and complicates safe mine-clearing operations; GPS jamming used in Gulf missile defences further disrupts AIS navigation.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The overlap between wartime AIS avoidance and pre-existing sanctions-evasion 'dark fleet' tactics complicates maritime risk monitoring and could mask illicit Iranian oil shipments during the transition period.
insights.locator: Mine clearing will also be difficult paragraph

insights.claim: Iran's newly established Persian Gulf Strait Authority has warned that ships not using its 'authorised routes' will not be guaranteed safe passage, and the IMO paused efforts to evacuate stranded seafarers after an attack on a cargo ship off Oman that the US blamed on Iran.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Iran's new sovereignty claims and the suspended IMO evacuation mission signal the ceasefire remains fragile, a live risk factor for oil-market volatility and shipping insurance premiums.

themes.theme: Who clears the mines: Iran, the US and allies

insights.claim: Point 5 of the US-Iran memorandum of understanding places demining responsibility on Tehran within 30 days, but Iran's forces are optimized for laying mines rather than removing them, and Iran alone may not be trusted to clear the mines by itself.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A 30-day MoU deadline that is likely unmet given Iran's laying-not-clearing force structure is a concrete near-term test of whether the ceasefire framework will hold or slip.
insights.quote: The traffic of commercial vessels will immediately start, and considering the need for removing the tactical and military obstacles and demining by the Islamic Republic of Iran will be instated within 30 days.
insights.locator: Point 5 of the US-Iran memorandum quote

insights.claim: Because Iran laid the mines, it holds crucial information on mine locations, types and arming settings, and its participation in demining could signal commitment to the peace process and reduce incentives to re-mine in future.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: This frames Iranian demining cooperation as a trust-building mechanism worth watching as a leading indicator of whether the broader US-Iran deal will hold.
insights.locator: Given that Iran laid the mines paragraph

insights.claim: The US has an arsenal of drones, explosive robots and helicopters for mine detection but currently only one ship available for mine clearing in the Middle East, and has historically relied on NATO allies to cover this capability gap, meaning it cannot demine the Strait alone.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The US's single-ship mine-clearing capacity in the region is a hard capability constraint that necessitates a multinational effort, extending the realistic timeline for full Strait reopening.
insights.locator: While the US has an arsenal paragraph

insights.claim: A UK-France proposed multinational coalition includes several European navies with specialist mine countermeasure forces; the UK has offered unmanned mine warfare capabilities paired with HMS Dragon for escort, while Germany, the Netherlands, Italy and the EU's EUNAVFOR Operation Aspides could also contribute.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: This names the specific coalition and assets (HMS Dragon, EUNAVFOR Aspides) likely to be deployed, giving investors and policymakers a concrete list of state actors to track for demining mission developments.
insights.locator: Other countries have a willingness paragraph

insights.claim: Mine clearance is often treated as a technical challenge, but in the Strait of Hormuz the greater obstacle is political: demining requires specialized vessels operating predictably in confined waters, which cannot work effectively unless all parties trust they will not become targets, making a sustained cessation of hostilities a prerequisite.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: This reframes demining timelines as a function of ceasefire durability rather than technical capability, meaning oil-market participants should track US-Iran diplomatic signals as the primary driver of Strait reopening speed, not just naval logistics.
insights.locator: The need for a political solution section

insights.claim: The current ceasefire and MoU provide only limited guarantees, and large-scale mine clearance has yet to begin, meaning security guarantees will likely need to be extended well beyond the current timeline for demining operations to succeed.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: This is an explicit forecast that the current ceasefire timeline is insufficient, implying markets should expect a longer, more gradual normalization of Strait of Hormuz shipping capacity than official deadlines suggest.
